calendar.matters:
MASTER CALENDAR JURY
Off Calendar Apr-22-2025 Set For Trial On Oct-05-26 At 09:30 A.M. In Dept 206

MOTION TO CONSOLIDATE ACTIONS OF CASE(S) CGC-24-619824 WITH CASE CGC-24-619796
Off Calendar May-12-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT MATTHEW ACCARRINO , AN INDIVIDUAL, FREEWHEEL LLC MOTION TO CONSOLIDATE ACTIONS OF CASE(S) CGC-24-619824 WITH CASE CGC-24-619796.

Defendants Matthew Accarrino and Freewheel LLC ' (MATTHEW ACCARRINO and FREEWHEEL LLC) motion to consolidate for all purposes (complete consolidation) is denied.

"There are two types of consolidation: a complete consolidation resulting in a single action, and a consolidation of separate actions for trial. Under the former procedure, which may be utilized where the parties are identical and the causes of action should have been joined, the pleadings are regarded as merged, one set of findings is made, and one judgment is rendered. In a consolidation for trial, the pleadings, verdicts, findings and judgments are kept separate; the actions are simply tried together for the sake of convenience and judicial economy." (Sanchez v. Superior Court (1988) 203 Cal.App.3d 1391, 1396.)

When ruling on a motion to consolidate the court should consider the common issues of law and fact, timeliness (trial date/discovery), complexity of the cases, and prejudice to the parties.

CGC-24-619796 involves an eleven-count complaint alleging wage and hour violations, retaliation, etc. with regard to plaintiff Blaquiere's employment at the Mattina restaurant. CGC-24-619824 involves a fourteen-count complaint alleging wage and hour violations, retaliation, disability discrimination, etc. while he worked at the SPQR restaurant. While there is an overlap in witnesses (e.g. plaintiff and Accarrino) complete consolidation is improper under Sanchez and would lead to an unwieldy action. Plaintiff's claims are distinct as to each employer and CGC-24-619796 has no issues regarding disability discrimination. Complete consolidation would lead to jury confusion, a byzantine summary judgment motion, etc. The issues of prejudice and confusion outweigh any judicial economy that will be furthered by consolidation.

The court orders the parties to coordinate discovery and other motion practice to prevent duplication of effort and to conserve judicial and party resources.

Order is signed.

Judge: Joseph M. Quinn, Clerk: M. Goodman, Not Reported. (302/JMQ)
